text: The surgeon examines a parastomal and midline hernia defect in a patient with a previous ilioconduit and radical cystectomy. The incarcerated small bowel is visible within the hernia, along with omentum, which the surgeon begins to remove from the midline defect.

The surgeon identifies the conduit and visualizes the ICG dye introduced via a Foley catheter. The conduit is visible without significant adhesions. The surgeon proceeds to remove additional incarcerated omentum from the hernia defect.

The surgeon prepares to place a large piece of mesh over the hernia site, removing the falciform ligament to ensure the mesh lies flat against the anterior abdominal wall without intervening fatty tissue. The Foley catheter is removed, and the mesh is positioned with no tension, allowing lateralization. The midline defect is closed with reduced pressure, achieving closure without tension.
The surgeon cinches down the mesh to ensure it is tight and secure, then proceeds to close the peristomal defect.

The surgeon measures the space and selects a twenty by twenty-five coated mesh for the hernia repair. After undocking, the mesh is positioned laparoscopically with tacks to secure it in place. The mesh is aligned at the midline hernia, with marks indicating placement on either side of the urostomy.
The surgeon positions the mesh in the midline to hold it in place for suturing, using two trans-facial sutures on the lateral aspect of the stoma. The mesh is secured above and around the stoma area.
The surgeon sews the mesh into place circumferentially, creating a mesh tunnel for the conduit. Sutures are placed close to the bowel to secure the mesh and potentially reduce friction.

The surgeon begins suturing on the cephalad side of the stoma, continuing the circumferential placement of sutures around the mesh. Two additional sutures are placed to complete the perimeter, securing the mesh in position.
